name: Conditional logic chains
description: 'Customer A buys apples; everyone who buys apples buys pears; ...' Deduce what must be true via modus ponens, the contrapositive, and disjunctive syllogism. Wro
primer:
## Conditional & categorical logic
Deduce only what is forced. Valid moves:
- **Modus ponens / chaining:** from A→B, B→C and A, conclude C.
- **Contrapositive:** A→B ≡ ¬B→¬A (the only valid 'negative' move).
- **Disjunctive syllogism:** from 'X or Y' (not both) and ¬X, conclude Y.

The wrong answers are the three classic fallacies:
- **Converse** (affirming the consequent): A→B and B ⇏ A.
- **Inverse** (denying the antecedent): A→B and ¬A ⇏ ¬B.
- **Undistributed middle:** 'all P are M' + 'some M are S' ⇏ 'some P are S'.

If nothing is forced either way, the answer is *cannot be determined*.

cards.term:
All of the following statements are true:
  - Everyone who buys apples also buys pears.
  - Everyone who buys pears also buys grapes.
  - Customer A buys apples.
Which one of the following MUST be true?
choices: Customer A does not buy grapes.
Customer A buys none of the three items.
Customer A buys apples but not pears.
Customer A buys grapes.
Anyone who buys grapes must also buy apples.
cards.definition: Correct: Customer A buys grapes. Chain by modus ponens: apples → pears → grapes, and A buys apples, so A buys grapes. 'Grapes → apples' is the converse (affirming the consequent) and doesn't follow.

cards.term:
Both of the following statements are true:
  - Every customer who buys milk also buys bread.
  - Customer B does not buy bread.
Which one of the following MUST be true?
choices: Customer B buys bread.
Customer B buys milk.
Anyone who does not buy milk also does not buy bread.
Customer B does not buy milk.
Customer B buys both milk and bread.
cards.definition: Correct: Customer B does not buy milk. Use the contrapositive of milk → bread, i.e. ¬bread → ¬milk. B avoids bread, so B avoids milk. 'No milk → no bread' is the inverse (denying the antecedent) and is invalid.

cards.term:
Both of the following statements are true:
  - Everyone who buys tea also buys honey.
  - Customer C buys honey.
Which one of the following MUST be true?
choices: Customer C does not buy honey.
Everyone who buys honey also buys tea.
It cannot be determined whether Customer C buys tea.
Customer C does not buy tea.
Customer C buys tea.
cards.definition: Correct: It cannot be determined whether C buys tea. The rule is tea → honey, not honey → tea, so buying honey says nothing about tea — concluding 'C buys tea' is affirming the consequent, and you can't conclude she doesn't either.

cards.term:
Both of the following statements are true:
  - No one who buys coffee buys cocoa.
  - Customer D buys coffee.
Which one of the following MUST be true?
choices: Customer D does not buy coffee.
Anyone who does not buy cocoa must buy coffee.
Customer D does not buy cocoa.
Customer D buys both coffee and cocoa.
Customer D buys cocoa.
cards.definition: Correct: Customer D does not buy cocoa. 'No coffee-buyer buys cocoa' = coffee → ¬cocoa. D buys coffee, so D avoids cocoa. The converse 'avoids cocoa → buys coffee' doesn't follow (one can avoid both).

cards.term:
All of the following statements are true:
  - Everyone who buys salmon also buys lemon.
  - No one who buys lemon buys cream.
  - Customer E buys salmon.
Which one of the following MUST be true?
choices: Customer E does not buy lemon.
Customer E buys both lemon and cream.
Customer E does not buy cream.
Anyone who buys cream also buys salmon.
Customer E buys cream.
cards.definition: Correct: Customer E does not buy cream. Chain: salmon → lemon → ¬cream, and E buys salmon, so E avoids cream. 'Buys cream → buys salmon' reverses the direction and isn't entailed.

cards.term:
All of the following statements are true:
  - Everyone who buys roses also buys tulips.
  - Customer F buys roses.
  - Customer G buys tulips.
Which one of the following MUST be true?
choices: Customer G buys neither roses nor tulips.
Customer F buys roses but not tulips.
Customer G does not buy tulips.
Customer F buys tulips.
Customer G buys roses.
cards.definition: Correct: Customer F buys tulips. F buys roses and roses → tulips, so F buys tulips. G buys tulips, but tulips → roses is the converse, so whether G buys roses is undetermined — the trap.

cards.term:
All of the following statements are true:
  - Every customer buys juice or water (at least one of the two).
  - No customer buys both juice and water.
  - Customer H does not buy juice.
Which one of the following MUST be true?
choices: Customer H buys water only if she also buys juice.
Customer H buys neither juice nor water.
Customer H buys both juice and water.
Customer H buys juice.
Customer H buys water.
cards.definition: Correct: Customer H buys water. 'Juice or water' with 'not both' = exactly one. H rules out juice, so by disjunctive syllogism H buys water.

cards.term:
All of the following statements are true:
  - Everyone who buys figs also buys dates.
  - Everyone who buys dates also buys nuts.
  - Customer J does not buy nuts.
Which one of the following MUST be FALSE?
choices: Customer J buys eggs.
Customer J does not buy dates.
Customer J buys neither figs nor nuts.
Customer J buys figs.
Customer J does not buy figs.
cards.definition: Correct (MUST be FALSE): Customer J buys figs. Contrapositive of figs → dates → nuts gives ¬nuts → ¬dates → ¬figs. J buys no nuts, so J cannot buy figs — that statement is necessarily false. (Eggs are unconstrained; the negative statements are forced true.)

cards.term:
All of the following statements are true:
  - Anyone who buys a newspaper buys a coffee.
  - Anyone who buys a coffee buys a muffin.
  - No one who buys a muffin buys a donut.
  - Customer K buys a newspaper.
Which one of the following MUST be true?
choices: Customer K does not buy a coffee.
Customer K buys a donut.
Customer K does not buy a donut.
Anyone who buys a donut buys a newspaper.
Customer K buys both a muffin and a donut.
cards.definition: Correct: Customer K does not buy a donut. Chain: newspaper → coffee → muffin → ¬donut, and K buys a newspaper, so K avoids a donut. 'Buys a donut → buys a newspaper' is an invalid converse.

cards.term:
All of the following statements are true:
  - Everyone who buys wine also buys cheese.
  - No one who buys cheese buys soda.
  - Customer L buys soda.
Which one of the following COULD be true (is consistent with the statements)?
choices: Customer L buys both soda and cheese.
Customer L buys bread.
Customer L buys cheese.
Customer L buys both wine and cheese.
Customer L buys wine.
cards.definition: Correct: Customer L buys bread. L buys soda; cheese → ¬soda gives soda → ¬cheese, so L has no cheese; wine → cheese then forces no wine. Wine and cheese are impossible, so among the options only the unconstrained 'buys bread' could be true.

cards.term:
Both of the following statements are true:
  - Every customer who buys organic produce also buys local produce.
  - Some customers who buy local produce also buy imported produce.
Which one of the following MUST be true?
choices: Every customer who buys organic produce also buys imported produce.
Some customer who buys organic produce also buys imported produce.
No customer who buys organic produce buys imported produce.
Every customer who buys imported produce buys organic produce.
It cannot be validly concluded that any organic-produce buyer buys imported produce.
cards.definition: Correct: It cannot be validly concluded that any organic buyer buys imported. Undistributed middle: organic ⊆ local, and some local buy imported, but those needn't be the organic ones. Counter-model: customer 1 = organic+local (no imported); customer 2 = local+imported (not organic) — both premises hold, yet no organic buyer buys imported.
